title: Step One
text: Who cares to admit complete defeat? Practically no one, of course. Every natural instinct rebels against the idea of personal powerlessness. It is truly awful to admit that, glass in hand, we have warped our minds into such an obsession for destructive drinking that only an act of Providence can remove it from us. No other kind of bankruptcy is like this one.

title: Step Two
text: The moment we face the forest of agnostics, atheists, or those who are completely indifferent, we see that we must find a Power greater than ourselves. AA does not demand that you believe in anything. It only asks that you be open-minded enough to believe that a Power greater than yourself exists. Upon this simple foundation, our recovery begins.

title: Step Three
text: Like a locked door, this step opens to a key. That key is willingness. Once we have key in hand, we find that we can open the door and step through into a new world. We make a decision to turn our will and our lives over to the care of God as we understand Him. This decision is the cornerstone of our recovery.

title: Step Four
text: We want to find out exactly how and when our natural desires have warped us. We want to look at our lives honestly, to see where pride, greed, anger, resentment, fear, and selfishness have led us astray. Making a searching and fearless moral inventory is the only way to clear away the wreckage of our past and discover our true selves.

title: Step Five
text: All of AA's Twelve Steps ask us to go contrary to our natural desires; they all deflate our egos. When it comes to ego deflation, few Steps are harder than Step Five. Admitting to God, to ourselves, and to another human being the exact nature of our wrongs is a humbling experience, but it is necessary for true freedom and sanity.

title: Step Six
text: This is the Step that separates the men from the boys. It is a step of preparation. We are asked to become entirely ready to have God remove all our defects of character. This requires us to look at our shortcomings honestly and ask ourselves if we are truly willing to let them go, even those we secretly cherish.

title: Step Seven
text: Humility is the foundation of all these steps. Without humility, we cannot make any real progress. In Step Seven, we humbly ask God to remove our shortcomings. This means acknowledging that we cannot overcome our character defects by willpower alone; we need a Power greater than ourselves to transform us.

title: Step Eight
text: We look backward and try to see where we were wrong. We make a list of all persons we have harmed and become willing to make amends to them all. This requires us to examine our relationships honestly, without making excuses or shifting the blame to others. We prepare ourselves to clear the slate.

title: Step Nine
text: Good judgment, a careful sense of timing, courage, and prudence are the qualities we need when we make amends in Step Nine. We make direct amends to those we have harmed, except when to do so would injure them or others. This requires us to put principles before our own comfort and face our past with integrity.

title: Step Ten
text: As we work this Step, we begin to put our recovery into daily practice. We continue to take personal inventory and when we are wrong promptly admit it. This is our daily maintenance step. It helps us stay aware of our motives, actions, and reactions, ensuring that we do not let old habits and resentments build up.

title: Step Eleven
text: Prayer and meditation are our principal means of conscious contact. We seek through prayer and meditation to improve our conscious contact with God as we understand Him, praying only for knowledge of His will for us and the power to carry that out. This step grounds us spiritually and guides our daily path.

title: Step Twelve
text: Having had a spiritual awakening as the result of these steps, we try to carry this message to alcoholics, and to practice these principles in all our affairs. This is the culmination of the program. We find joy in service, carrying hope to those who still suffer, and integrating recovery into every aspect of our lives.

title: Tradition One
text: Our common welfare should come first; personal recovery depends upon AA unity. Unity is the bedrock of our fellowship. Without unity, individual recovery would be impossible. We must place the common welfare of the group and the fellowship above our personal desires and opinions.

title: Tradition Two
text: For our group purpose there is but one ultimate authority—a loving God as He may express Himself in our group conscience. Our leaders are but trusted servants; they do not govern. True leadership in AA is based on humility, service, and listening to the group conscience, rather than personal power or authority.

title: Tradition Three
text: The only requirement for AA membership is a desire to stop drinking. This tradition ensures that our doors are open to anyone who suffers from alcoholism, regardless of their background, beliefs, or lifestyle. We exclude no one who wants to recover.

title: Tradition Four
text: Each group should be autonomous except in matters affecting other groups or AA as a whole. This freedom allows groups to structure meetings in ways that best serve their members, while protecting the integrity and unity of the global fellowship.

title: Tradition Five
text: Each group has but one primary purpose—to carry its message to the alcoholic who still suffers. This single-minded focus is our strength. We must not be distracted by outside enterprises, political debates, or other causes; our mission is to carry the message of recovery.

title: Tradition Six
text: An AA group ought never endorse, finance, or lend the AA name to any related facility or outside enterprise, lest problems of money, property, and prestige divert us from our primary purpose. This keeps AA independent and free from commercial or organizational entanglements.

title: Tradition Seven
text: Every AA group ought to be fully self-supporting, declining outside contributions. This principle of self-reliance ensures that AA remains free from outside influence and control. We pay our own way and do not accept money from non-members.

title: Tradition Eight
text: Alcoholics Anonymous should remain forever nonprofessional, but our service centers may employ special workers. Twelfth-step work is always a free gift of love, never done for money. However, our offices and service centers require hired help to function effectively.

title: Tradition Nine
text: AA, as such, ought never be organized; but we may create service boards or committees directly responsible to those they serve. We have no hierarchies or commanding authorities in AA. Our service structures exist only to facilitate group communication and carry the message.

title: Tradition Ten
text: Alcoholics Anonymous has no opinion on outside issues; hence the AA name ought never be drawn into public controversy. This protection keeps AA unified and prevents us from being divided by political, social, or religious debates that do not concern our primary purpose.

title: Tradition Eleven
text: Our public relations policy is based on attraction rather than promotion; we need always maintain personal anonymity at the level of press, radio, and films. We do not advertise or brag about AA's successes; we let the results speak for themselves. We protect the anonymity of our members.

title: Tradition Twelve
text: Anonymity is the spiritual foundation of all our traditions, ever reminding us to place principles before personalities. Humility and selfless service are the core of our spiritual life. We focus on the principles of recovery rather than personal fame or credit.
